title: Ratification of the US Constitution
description: Exercise on the compromises necessary for ratification of the Constitution
topic_path: How was the Constitution designed, debated, and ratified?

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The House of Representatives shall be composed of Members chosen every second Year by the People of the several States . . . The number of Representatives shall not exceed one for every thirty Thousand, but each State shall have at Least one Representative . . . 

>“SECTION 3
>“The Senate of the United States shall be composed of two Senators from each State, chosen by the Legislature thereof, for six Years; and each Senator shall have one Vote.”

>Sections 2 and 3 of the United States Constitution 

**The excerpt above is the result of which compromise in the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Great (Connecticut) Compromise
Compromise on the Bill of Rights
Compromise on the Electoral College
Three-Fifths Compromise

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Three-Fifths Compromise is best defined as which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: For every five enslaved Africans, three would be counted for representation
A bill can only be passed with three-fifths majority of both the House and the Senate
Three-fifths of the Senate would be up for reelection every two years
Three-fifths of United States territory in 1787 would become slave states in 1800

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best explains the compromise behind the creation of the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was a compromise between big and small states so that states with a larger population cannot control presidential elections
It was a compromise between slave and free states over how enslaved Africans would be counted for representation
It was a compromise that dictated presidential impeachment procedures
It was a compromise between Federalists and Anti-Federalists on the power of the Presidency

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following procedures results in the addition of an amendment to the Constitution?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Two-thirds of state legislatures propose a Constitution Amendment Bill, which is ratified by three-fourths of the states
The House and the Senate pass a Constitution Amendment Bill by a majority and the Supreme Court approves the bill
The House and the Senate pass a Constitution Amendment Bill by a simple majority
Two-thirds of state legislatures propose a Constitution Amendment Bill which is ratified by three-fourths of the House and the Senate

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why have there been relatively few constitutional amendments?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A Constitution Amendment Bill requires more than a simple majority to be ratified
Once a Constitution Amendment Bill is proposed there is 6 months time limit for approval
They must be voted on as a referendum in three-fourths state legislatures
They must be approved by both the Supreme Court and the President

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Framers decide to include Article V of the Constitution, detailing an amendment process?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Concerns about protecting self-government
Concerns about the central government becoming too powerful
Concerns over tyranny of the majority
Concerns over the executive branch becoming too powerful

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Because of our two-party system, voters often find themselves voting for the ‘lesser of two evils,’ rather than a candidate they really feel would do the best job. . . .
 
>"Since most states distribute their electoral votes on a winner-take-all basis, the smaller party has no chance to gain support without seeming to take this support from one of the major parties. Few people will support a party that never wins, especially when they are supporting that party at the possible expense of their least favorite candidate taking power.”

*Source: [FairVote.org](https://archive3.fairvote.org/reforms/national-popular-vote/the-electoral-college/problems-with-the-electoral-college/)*

**Which of the following governmental policies would the author of this passage most likely support?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Reforming or eliminating the Electoral College
Requiring all states to allocate electoral votes on a winner-take-all basis
Preventing third party “spoilers” from running in national elections
Amending the Constitution to ban political parties

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Given the increasing difference in size between the largest and smallest states, combined with other changes in the American political context[,] any effects of Senate apportionment are greater today than in the past. By reference to the one person, one vote standard, the Senate is the most malapportioned legislature in the world . . . Currently Wyoming, with fewer than half a million residents, enjoys the same level of representation as California, with more than 30 million.”

> -Frances E. Lee and Bruce I. Oppenheimer, *Sizing Up the Senate: The Unequal Consequences of Equal Representation,* 1999 

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Great Compromise failed to anticipate growing population differences in small and large states, leaving matters unresolved
The Electoral College failed to anticipate growing population differences in small and large states, leading to unequal representation in the House of Representatives
The Three-Fifths Compromise gave small states too much representation in the Senate, leading to more filibusters
The amendment process detailed in Article V gave large states an unfair advantage in ratifying new amendments in the Senate

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why are the compromises that were necessary to secure ratification of the Constitution still debated today?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They left some matters unresolved
They created strict requirements to change the Constitution
The compromises did not account for changes in population
The compromises violated states’ rights

assessment_items.question_content:
A member of the House of Representatives proposes a Constitutional amendment that would force the president and Congress to balance the budget every year.

**Which of the following accurately describes the amendment process?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The bill must pass both the House and the Senate with a two-thirds majority
The bill must pass both the House and the Senate with a simple majority
The bill must pass the House and the Senate with a two-thirds majority and be approved by the Supreme Court
The bill must pass both the House and the Senate with a three-fourths majority

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of a present-day constitutional issue about democracy and governmental power?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: FBI agents being able to obtain phone records without a judge’s approval
Congress passing a bill that will increase taxes on the top 1% of earners
Congress passing a law requiring restrooms in public buildings to be equipped with baby changing facilities
Reducing the corporate tax rate as part of the 2017 tax reform bill

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following policies is an example of a present-day debate about the balance of power between the federal government and state governments?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Congress passing the Every Student Succeeds (ESSA) Act that decreased the number of standardized tests
Congress passing a law to name a US Postal service office located in New Jersey the “James C. ‘Billy’ Johnson Post Office Building”
Congress passing a law limiting the weight of trucks on interstate highways
Congress passing a law replacing Andrew Jackson with Harriet Tubman on the 20 dollar bill

assessment_items.question_content:
After the September 11 attacks, Congress passed the USA PATRIOT Act, which gave the federal government expanded powers to monitor communications and investigate suspected terrorist activity. Supporters argued the law was essential for national security, while critics claimed it violated individual rights.

**Which constitutional debate is most directly reflected in the scenario above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Balance between federal power and individual civil liberties
Separation of powers between the executive and judicial branches
Balance of power between the federal government and state governments
Checks and balances between the executive and legislative branches of government

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which issue from the Constitutional Convention is most similar to present-day debates about government surveillance and civil liberties?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How to balance individual rights with national authority
Whether to count enslaved people toward representation
How to structure the national court system
Whether the president should be chosen by the legislature

assessment_items.question_content:
A federal education law requires all states to use the same standardized test to measure student performance. Some state officials argue that the law violates their authority to set educational policy. Supporters say it ensures equal opportunity for students nationwide.

**Which best explains the constitutional principle being debated in this scenario?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Division of power between national and state governments
Checks and balances between branches of the federal government
The Supreme Court’s authority to interpret education law
Popular sovereignty and the will of the people
